sentences.text: My name is Reva Kibort.
R-E-V-A K-I-B-O-R-T.
I was born in Warsaw, Poland.
W-A-R-S-A-W.
Poland-- P-O-L-A-N-D.
My maiden name is Reva Mandelbaum.
M-A-N-D-E-L-B-A-U-M.
I was born March 4, 1933.
In Warsaw.
Well, my earliest memory is, of course, being a little girl and playing with my little, class-- not classmates-- little friends that lived in the same courtyard where I lived.
And I was a very poor little girl.
We didn't have very much.
And I remember playing with a little rag dolls and with little-- whatever we could find on the streets to play with.
And I had a family.
I had a mother and a father, of course.
I had five sisters and a brother, and they were all very loving to me.
And I had a pretty normal life.
It wasn't the most fantastic materialistically, anyway.
But it was a wonderful, warm relationship that we had with the whole family.
My father was a shoemaker, but he did not repair old shoes.
He made new shoes to order.
In other words, if he had an order from a company or from a store, he would make these shoes for them.
And it depended a lot on the season.
If he had a good season, we ate well.
If he didn't have a good season, we didn't eat so well.
But we-- we really had a very happy home.
The home was always happy.
And my mother really tried her best to have a warm home.
No.
My father worked at home.
He had people.
He had two men come in to help him work.
The workshop was at home.
And I remember my father, one year especially, 1938-- that year, somehow or other, really sticks out in my mind.
Because for Passover, no matter how poor you were, you always, somehow or other, had shoes to wear and dresses to wear-- new outfits.
And I remember that my father made a beautiful pair of shoes for me.
They were navy blue and red little tassels.
And I remember-- I will always remember those little shoes.
[SIGHS]
Those shoes, actually-- those shoes, really, carried me through the whole two years of the Warsaw Ghetto.
My mother was mainly a homemaker.
She worked very, very hard at home.
And she tried to make everybody happy, I guess.
No, the shoes I wore.
I wore them throughout the ghetto.
But I will always remember them.
Of course, as my feet grew, of course, I had to get rid of them.
Because they just didn't fit.
But I will always remember those little shoes.
[SIGHS]
Yes, yes.
I was the youngest at that time, because I had a younger little sister who had died in 1938 of kidney failure.
But I was the youngest at that point.
And everybody was nice to me.
You know, being the youngest child in a family, everybody sort of tried to take care of you.
And-- and then, of course, in 1939, when the Germans came into Warsaw, they were bombing the city.
And I remember that very vividly, too.
Because we tried-- we ran from one building to the next.
And my mother would drag me by my hand, you know.
Because as they were throwing the bombs from one building to the next, you tried to avoid the fires, of course.
And-- and at that time, my father was killed-- was killed September 14, 1939 when the Germans tried to occupy Poland.
And being, you know, that I was a little girl, everybody, somehow or other, tried to take care of me, you know.
Well, oh, well, the main holiday that I remember, the most important holiday, is Passover.
And I still have a great love for Passover, because I still make a very festive time of it even now.
I remember about Passover that we always used to sit around the table for the Seders-- all my sisters and my brother and my parents.
And somehow or other by the time midnight came when the Seder was always over, I was always asleep.
And they had to wake me up.
And especially, when Elijah-- when we were letting Elijah into the house, they woke me up.
And it's time to get up.
Our Seder was long, you know.
And it was such a beautiful time.
And I loved it.
I remember, once, going with my father to buy the matzah.
And we used to buy the matzah.
And they came in round brown bags.
They were round matzah.
And I remember my-- my father putting it away.
We had at the house-- a wardrobe, you know.
We did not have built-in closets, so we had wardrobes.
And he put the matzah away on top.
And he said, you cannot touch it, you know, till Pesach.
And that-- that, somehow or other, still sticks out in my mind.
I remember going for him and buying the matzah.
It was a-- it was a wonderful time.
And of course, you couldn't eat certain foods.
You know, you had to eat-- when you think about it, now, that you had to eat schmaltz with matzah.
You couldn't have butter.
You couldn't have milk.
Nowadays, everything is so modernized that you can eat almost anything on Passover.
But it was a festive time.
And Rosh Hashanah, too-- every holiday was a very, very festive time.
We made it-- my parents tried to make it very festive for all of us.
And that's why the holidays are so important to me.
And I have made them important for my children, too.
No, unfortunately.
We had a lot of relatives.
But we couldn't get together, because everybody had their own Seders, you know?
And people lived far away.
So that would mean you would have to drive on the holiday.
And a lot of people would not drive.
Actually, we didn't have cars, you know.
It was a matter of taking a streetcar or something else.
But no, it was mainly our own family.
And then during the holiday, we would walk.
Either-- if it was walking distance, we would walk to their home.
But it was always wonderful-- just a wonderful time.
Well, Friday night, I remember, again, it was always food to eat-- challahs, wonderful soups, and fish.
My mother made the most delicious gefilte fish.
And it was very, very warm.
You know, during the week, it was an ordinary day.
People went to work.
And of course, my father, like I said, worked at home.
But when Shabbos came, you knew it was Shabbat.
You just knew it.
Because the house was clean.
The house was scrubbed.
And there was a white tablecloth.
And my mother made special food.
It was wonderful.
It was a warm aroma.
It was just-- it's not only the aroma that you felt, it's the whole warmth, the whole feeling of being Shabbat.
And by-- by no means-- we were not considered very religious people.
I mean, my mother kept a kosher home, my father did not work on Shabbat.
He did not smoke on Shabbat.
When he ate, he put on a hat.
But yet, by nowadays, by-- we were not considered an Orthodox family, but an observant family, a traditional family.
And it was wonderful.
It was wonderful.
Thank God that I have all these good memories before all the bad ones started coming in.
Brother.
Well, they would come home.
And mostly, like any other students, they would have to do homework.
And they would talk about it.
But they sort of left me to myself, because I didn't go to school yet.
And I was playing with other things, collecting little stones outside or, you know, things like that.
They really didn't-- they didn't pay that much attention to me at that time when they were in school.
Because they had their own friends.
They had their own things that they were busy with.
Her name was Pearl [INAUDIBLE]
Rochenshwald, [INAUDIBLE] Rochenshwald.
Chaim Leibesh Mandelbaum.
Yes.
No, no.
The first thing I remember about the Germans-- actually, I remember when they arrived into Warsaw.
And in the beginning, you know, it wasn't so bad.
Because I remember standing in line for food.
And they were dishing out soup and giving people bread to eat.
And I-- we, you know, all thought, you know, they were nice.
They soldiers seemed very nice.
Like I said, they fed us.
And nobody thought that this hell is waiting for us.
Nobody could have possibly imagined what these people would do to us, nobody.
So this was my first memory of the Germans.
And, you know, things were pretty normal for about a year, I would say.
Till 1940, things were normal.
People were going on doing their own thing.
And then, hell broke loose, like we say.
Well, the bombings-- well, you know, my father was very interested in politics.
And I think he knew a lot what was going on.
And they were talking about it.
The adults were talking about it.
But, you know, being a child, you don't pay attention to it.
You just don't listen.
After all, I was only like six, seven years old.
But I know that my father was very interested in politics.
And he used to talk with the other men and saying what's going to happen and why it's happening and all of that.
And then, in 1939, actually, a lot of people were trying to escape to Russia.
They were trying to leave the country.
Huh?
Yes, people that my father knew.
And as a matter of fact, my uncle-- I have an uncle who left for Russia, too.
And he came, and he asked my father to leave.
He said, why don't we all go?
And of course, being that my father had a family of six children and a wife, he just could not leave.
He said, I cannot leave the family.
And it will be very difficult to-- to go.
As it turned out, one of my other uncles-- two uncles actually left.
And one of them was killed on the way.
Whether he was killed by the Russians or the Polish people, we don't know.
But he was killed.
He never returned.
And this other uncle did survive.
He came to-- he went to Russia.
And he did survive the war.
But my father just would not leave.
He just wouldn't.
Oh, yes.
People were talking about it.
But, you know, it was already 1939.
It was, actually, pretty late at that point to leave.
If you could have left earlier, of course, you would have been much safer.
But 1939 was already pretty late.
Well, it happened like I said.
My father was killed September 1940-- '39 when they were bombing Warsaw.
It was Rosh Hashanah-- eve of Rosh Hashanah, actually.
And my sister, my older sister, Hannah, had gone to our other house.
Because during the bombing, we had moved in with an aunt.
And she had gone to the house to check out on our house where we lived during the regular time.
And she went to pick up some-- not wine.
I think my mother had put up some cherries, you know, that-- for the holidays.
And my sister wanted to bring it home.
And so she was going to this house where we were living.
And before we knew it, the bombs started coming.
The Germans were bombing the city again.
And my father ran out of the house trying to see in which directions the bombs were coming.
And he noticed a lot of smoke and fire coming from the direction where our house was.
And he ran.
He wanted to run towards that area, towards our house.
Because he kept saying that his daughter is there, his daughter.
My sister Hannah was there.
And the next thing we knew, my father never returned.
It was nighttime.
And my father did not return.
And the following morning, my mother and my older sister went out looking for my father.
And because there were a lot of dead people on the streets that had died during the bombing.
And an aunt of mine-- which was also her name Hannah, my father's older sister-- went out, also, looking for him.
And they, finally, found him on the street.
And when they found him, he was-- just his forehead was a little bit scratched.
He had no shoes on, because somebody must have taken his shoes off.
And all the identification that he had with him, everything was taken away.
And he didn't look as though-- he could have either been trampled to death, or he could have had a heart attack.
We don't know.
He was only 42 years old.
So it's very unlikely that he had a heart attack.
It, more than likely, was that, either, he was trampled because people were running.
And they found his body.
And then, next day, they had to bury him because according, of course, to the Jewish law, you have to bury them right away.
But unfortunately, here, they had hundreds of other people who had perished that day from the bombs.
And they took my father to the cemetery.
And he was buried in a grave with among, maybe, 500 people or more.
Because they were not-- they did not have enough time, because the bombs were still-- the Germans were still bombing the city.
So they had to bury the dead as fast as they possibly could.
And therefore, they buried him in a large grave.
But I did not go to the cemetery, because my mother thought I was too little.
And she didn't take me.
So I, actually, never have seen it.
I have not been.
At that time, I did not go to the cemetery.
My sister, my older-- my oldest three sisters went-- my sisters Marsha, Hannah, and Mindall.
And I think, maybe, my other sisters went to the cemetery.
The other young children they didn't go.
Oh, yes.
My sister Hannah survived, absolutely.
She came home.
She was saved.
And my father, he had a premonition that something was wrong.
And he went out to-- to look for his child like any normal father would.
And he died.
He paid for his with his life.
Well, after the bombing was over, we went back to our house.
And we lived there for a while.
It was very, very hard for my mom.
[LAUGHS] I remember my sisters-- you know, when you don't have money and you don't have food, you will do anything.
So my sisters used to go to the to the street-- not streetcar-- to the railroad where they were bringing in coal.
And we would steal the coal-- we would catch on the-- on the-- on the train.
And then, we would sell it.
And then, of course, some of it we would keep in the house to keep us warm.
And you would do all sorts of things, you know.
And my mother, somehow or other, tried to cope.
They-- we had very, very little food-- very little food.
And that's how I-- that's how we managed.
But at that time, my sisters still were going to school.
Life turned to pretty normal.
And then, of course, in 1940 again, that's when all the things started again.
Right.
It was just a few months that things like that-- you know.
Yeah, till about half a year, I would say.
Well, I-- you know, I don't remember it clearly.
As I said, being that I was so young, I don't remember a lot of the things what exactly.
I can only remember that we were told that we had to move out of that area-- that we were being moved to a-- a ghetto.
I had never heard of the word ghetto till the Warsaw Ghetto, believe it or not.
And what they did-- the Jewish community was organizing.
They had organized a Judenrat.
They had Jewish policeman.
And an order was simply put out that you have to move within a certain area.
And that's what happened.
We moved to [NON-ENGLISH].
And my aunt lived there-- my aunt.
I had a very well-to-do aunt.
And she had suggested that we move in with her.
And-- and it just so happened the area where she was at was considered a part of the ghetto.
And once they organized the ghetto, of course, the Jewish children were not allowed to go to school.
My sisters all had to quit school.
And our whole life was disrupted.
Everything that we had-- you could only take with you whatever you can carry.
Everything, the furniture, whatever you had, everything had to be left behind.
You could not take anything with you.
And what they tried to do is tried to put in so many Jews into a certain square-- not square block.
It was bigger than a square block.
But that was mainly their objective, to put everybody together in one place.
And it was very, very hard in the ghetto, very, very difficult.
In the beginning, it wasn't so bad, because they were still giving out pieces of bread.
And they had rations, of course.
Well, I-- I only remembered carrying something on my back-- probably all-- the all clothes, whatever I had.
And believe me, I did not have very much.
Because as I said to you before, we were not a well-to-do family.
So we just carried on our backs whatever we could.
And then--
I didn't have anything really special for me, nothing.
There really wasn't anything other than the clothes that I was wearing and maybe a few extra items.
We took pictures.
We took a lot of pictures.
My mother took pictures, my father.
Whatever of value that we did have, everything we did take with us.
Things that we could carry, we took.
And once we got in the ghetto, of course, what they tried to do, they put in a lot of families in one apartment.
It wasn't just one family.
It was two or three families living in one apartment with the three rooms or four rooms.
But we were lucky, because we lived with our aunt.
And of course, being that we were seven of us, plus my aunt and her child-- and she's the one who lost her husband.
Her husband is the one who went to-- who tried to go away.
And he's the one who was killed.
And then, things got worse.
As the ghetto--
Ethel.
My Aunt Ethel.
And things really became very, very bad in the ghetto, because it started with starvation.
The Germans tried to give us less food.
They started taking people out for work.
At least, they claimed that people were going to work.
And the first thing they did, of course, they took out the young healthy men.
Then they started with the-- the healthy young women.
And eventually, you ended up with the ghetto was old women and children.
And the most thing that I remember about the ghetto was the starvation, the dirt, the filth, the sickness.
My mother had to pull through six children with typhus.
We all got sick.
Somehow or other, God must have watched over my mother, because she never got sick.
And she had to pull all-- all six of us through that sickness.
The death on the streets, every morning, where you get up, you'd find dead corpse on the street.
And people were trying to take off their clothes, because they needed the clothes themselves for warmth.
There was no dignity left anymore in the ghetto.
People became-- people that normally would smile at you, would be nice to you, became vicious, became very, very mean.
Because all they were thinking about is themselves and how are they going to survive.
It was unbelievable.
It was so bad in the ghetto.
And, of course, everywhere you went, the Germans were there, you know, with the German-- the dogs, the German shepherds.
It was very bad.
And I remember the hunger that I suffered.
And I used to come into the house, and my mother would say to me-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] [SOBBING] she said to me, go out and play.
She said, if you will play and sing a song, you'll forget that you're hungry.
And my mother was only 39 years old.
And you know what happened?
When I turned 39 years old, when I was 39, and I remember going to services.
And I spoke to my rabbi that morning.
And I said, you know what?
I'm 39 years old today.
And my mother was 39 when she was killed.
And the biggest pain that I have is remembering when-- [CRYING] when she told me she cannot feed me, because she had no food.
And the pain, the pain she must have had to tell me, I have nothing to give you.
You'll just have to go out and try to sing.
And maybe you'll forget how hungry you were.
And-- [CRYING] that pain has always been with me.
I just cannot forget the pain she must have had to have to tell a child that I have no food for you.
Because I always put myself in her shoes, what I would have to do if it were my children.
It was very, very bad, the ghetto.
And then, on top of everything else, I saw my own grandmother die of starvation-- my father's mother die.
I had two little cousins who I adored.
Her name was [INAUDIBLE] and Hershel.
And they were such beautiful children.
And they died of starvation.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it was just terrible.
The ghetto was so bad.
I mean, they took everything away from us, everything.
All the dignity that we ever had, they took away.
It was just terrible.
OK.
